title: Citizenship and human rights: ideals and actualities
abstract: In her Debate article (Soysal 2012), Yasemin Soysal outlines a now familiar argument. As Jessop (1999) cogently puts it, challenges to the Keynesian Welfare National State model driven by a changing global division of labour have produced a Schumpeterian Workfare Postnational Regime of permanent innovation, flexibility and competitiveness. These broad structural changes are commonly argued to have been accompanied, and in part facilitated, by a shift in the governance of welfare and a reconstitution of the welfare subject. It is therefore hard to disagree with the main thrust of Soysal's argument, and she brings a wealth of material to bear in mapping the way these trends play out at the European level, with respect to welfare, migration, and the model of the ‘good citizen’. Given this context, it is not surprising to find that the work of T.H. Marshall is a key referent for Soysal, and especially his essay ‘Citizenship and Social Class’, (Marshall 1992 [1950]) which famously outlines the development of British citizenship through the chronological unfolding of three sets of rights; civil, political and social. This process, Marshall argued, was to culminate in social provisions which guaranteed: ‘a modicum of economic welfare and security . . . (and) the right to share to the full in the social heritage and live the life of a civilised being . . . ’ (Marshall 1992: 8). One key feature of his model lies in the assertion that ‘equality of status is more important than equality of income’ (1992: 33), and this amounts to a view of membership as a statement of individual social worth; the essay thus standing as a precursor to contemporary work which takes ‘recognition’ as its focal point. My first question turns on the status of Marshall's model in Soysal's article, and my point here is not to contest the substance of her argument, but rather to pause and think about how we conceptualize and measure change, and to speculate a little about the relationship between the normative and the empirical in this process. T.H. Marshall takes up a hypothesis present in the work of Alfred Marshall, who postulates that: ‘there is a kind of basic human equality associated with the concept of full membership of a community’ (Marshall 1992: 6) which is not inconsistent with the inequalities of social class. One aim of Marshall's essay was to test how far this hypothesis held true in Britain at his own time of writing. His answer was that ‘the preservation of economic inequalities has been made more difficult by the enrichment of the status of citizenship’ (1992: 45), and he saw this ‘modern drive towards social equality’ (1992: 7) as the latest phase in the evolution of citizenship. However, he also observed that a ‘stratified status system is creeping into citizenship’ (1992: 40) and that ‘conflicts within our social system are becoming too sharp . . . ’. (1992: 49) So I have often felt that Marshall's essay is itself unclear as to whether it refers to a process already brought to full conclusion, or whether it sets out an ideal version of what that conclusion should be. My question then is, when we document change with reference to Marshall's conception of social citizenship are we talking about change as measured by reference to an ideal, or change as measured by reference to an already functioning system of full social inclusion, and why does this matter? Soysal seems to assume the latter; at least she states with respect to Marshall's model that ‘European welfare states have successfully followed this formula . . . ’ (2012: 2), though she signals elsewhere in the article that some scholars might question this assertion. In Britain, which was Marshall's case of reference, it seems unlikely that the expressed ideal was ever fully actualized (Morris with Llewellyn 1991); the setting of the benefit rate by Beveridge fell significantly short of the subsistence levels recommended by Rowntree, and has always been bedevilled by a concern to maintain the work incentive. Furthermore, benefits conferred through the system of National Insurance had always to be supplemented by National Assistance, this latter being conditional on the indignities of means testing. By the 1970s the system was heavily dependent on discretionary and exceptional needs payments, and a requirement for the unemployed to be actively seeking work was certainly in place by the late 1960s – sometime before the changes with which Soysal is concerned. Space precludes a treatment here of the considerable historical and geographical variation across Europe, which challenges any uniform picture, but I focus on Britain as the case on which Marshall's model was ostensibly based. So it is doubtful that the Marshallian ideal of equal status and social worth secured through social provisions was ever achieved; a stigma has always attached to benefit dependence for the unemployed, and conditionality has long been built into its receipt. I raise these matters because they point to a problem for the measurement and conceptualization of social change, and perhaps more particularly for the point at which we can identify a qualitative shift, rather than an intensification of already apparent tendencies which are endemic to the system as a whole. This prompts a further question as to the significance of the political discourse which has accompanied what I would certainly recognize as increased conditionality within the welfare system (Morris 2007). Blair's new contract for welfare (DSS 1998: Cm 3805) and Cameron's austerity regime play well against images of a lax and over-generous welfare system, and carry an ideological force which is possibly as significant as the changes themselves. The tendency for politicians to construct a problem (e.g. benefit abuse) with which they can claim to be actively struggling is well documented (Schneider and Ingram 1993), and in this context, the significance of Marshall's model takes on a different cast – as providing a counter-rhetoric which does not necessarily rest on an ever fully achieved golden age of social citizenship. We see this dynamic (and its gendered dimensions) played out in debate about an emergent ‘underclass’ (Morris 1994, 2006). But this is to raise another question; that of the role and treatment of normative ideals in sociological analysis, to which I return below. I would like first to turn to the conceptualization of rights, and the role they can play in the governance of society. Rights are rarely simply one-sided and whenever there is the granting of a right there is usually an opportunity for surveillance or control. David Lockwood's (1996) work is enormously helpful in addressing these possibilities and in setting out a framework for what we might consider to be a nascent sociology of rights; it provides an important supplement to Soysal's argument. Lockwood starts from the assumption that the ‘institutional unity’ of citizenship, market and bureaucracy is central to the delivery of social cohesion, but he reverses Marshall's model by proposing that: ‘the ethos and practice of citizenship is at least as likely as class relations to structure group interests and thereby fields of conflict and discontent’ (Lockwood 1996: 536). This he argues, is because citizenship remains an ideal whose ‘actualisation is always less than complete’ (Lockwood 1996: 536). The inequalities of citizenship are then argued to operate along two axes – the presence or absence of a right, and the possession of moral or material resources, the latter exerting a positive or negative effect on the actual enjoyment of a right. Although Lockwood's article is ostensibly concerned with inequalities generated by the functioning of citizenship, it admirably lends itself to a consideration of migrant rights. It can therefore inform analysis both of the way citizenship can be stratified by the disciplinary and control measures which accompany welfare claims, and the way migrants can be stratified by their purpose of entry and by the rights accordingly granted or denied to different categories. The existence of a blue card for highly skilled workers mentioned by Soysal is one example, as is the denial of progression to settlement for low skilled temporary migrant labour. We should also note that aspects of civic stratification are apparent in provisions and procedures for handling asylum claims. Furthermore, while the system of civic stratification operates in different ways and to different effect on citizens and migrants, there is a clear linkage between the two. Thus, attempts to force the indigenous unemployed into low paid, insecure work shuts down opportunities for low skilled migrants and/or forces them onto the lowest rung on the civic stratification ladder, that of undocumented worker. One obvious answer to Soysal's question ‘what do rights do?’ is therefore that they stratify, discipline and divide – often with reference to national interests, though they do not need to be tied to an overt project of collective national identity to perform this function. However, the relationship between the two different axes in Lockwood's schema brings us closer to my question about the role and treatment of ‘ideal’ conceptions of rights and citizens in sociological analysis. Lockwood's picture of how civic stratification functions is dynamic, as already implied by the argument that actualization of the ideal of citizenship is always less than complete. The driving force for this dynamism is to be found in the influence of what Lockwood terms moral and material resources, which can also be linked to conceptions of the ‘good citizen’. It is apparent from the argument so far that the complex of rights is open to manipulation by the state in pursuit of policy goals, and this suggests a close connection between rights and controls. However, there is also scope for a link between formal entitlement and informal status or social standing, whereby the possession of a right confirms or acknowledges social standing (or moral worth in Lockwood's terms), and the denial of a right undermines it. In combination, these two points suggest a theory of change whereby moral or material resources enhance entitlement, and may also underpin social mobilizations which seek to claim new rights for a given group. These arguments, however, must also logically entail the possibility of a reverse dynamic, whereby the discrediting of a given group serves as a prelude to reducing their rights.1 Estimations of moral worth or social standing will be inevitably tied to notions of the ‘good citizen’ in this process. This returns us to a consideration of the political discourse which has accompanied enhanced conditionality in welfare as perhaps indicative of the desire to delegitimize a more expansive and inclusive system. In publicizing the more punitive aspects of provision it can play a deleterious role for public perceptions of claimants' ‘social worth’, and Rodger (2003) has highlighted the significance of such ‘social steering’ in shaping public feelings about the welfare state. Similar processes are at work in the orchestration of public perceptions of trans-national migration, and the effect in both cases may be captured by the notion of ‘mis-recognition’ (eg. Fraser 2001). Conversely, where the granting of rights acts as a conferral of moral worth or social standing we may speak of ‘recognition’ (Honneth 1995). Kompridis (2008: 305) has suggested that: ‘Whatever recognition is . . . it seems to figure importantly in a process of re-education, in an alteration of our cognitive orientations and normative expectations’, and this opens the way to thinking about the role of normative models in sociological analysis. Sociologists tend to engage both in analysing public orientations and expectations, and in advancing alternatives (as in Marshall's essay), so another answer to the question of ‘what rights do’ is that they present a site of social struggle, and concretize often contested visions of society (e.g. Morris 2009a). Such argument highlights a certain indeterminacy or unfixity in both the content and boundary of rights (as noted by Soysal and others), and this has been argued to be a particular feature of unfolding areas of law, such as universal human rights (Dworkin 2005). I now turn to the question of the relationship between citizenship and universal human rights. Soysal's article raises the interesting and important question of the relationship between citizenship and human rights, and correctly remarks on their entangled histories. Marshall delivered the lecture on which his essay is based in 1949, three years after the National Insurance Act, one year after the National Assistance Act, and one year after the adoption of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights by the UN General Assembly. It is hard to imagine this Declaration was not in his mind as he wrote, and his division of the rights of citizenship into civil, political and social prefigures the two key International Covenants which together with the Declaration made up what is termed the International Bill of Rights, dealing respectively with civil, political and social rights, and with economic, social and cultural rights (both adopted in 1966). So one understanding of citizenship is that it provides the principle vehicle through which most people access their universal human rights, for as Donnelly (2003) has remarked, many such rights are made available through lower level provisions not normally requiring recourse to international law. So I agree with Soysal that there is no overt opposition, or dichotomy as she puts it, between citizenship and human rights, but I wonder if this notion of ‘dichotomy’ is a correct representation of contemporary scholarship? Certainly Habermas writes of the Janus-faced nature of the nation state, by which he means: ‘the tension between the universalism of an egalitarian legal community and the particularism of a community united by historical destiny . . . ’ (Habermas 1998:115), while Benhabib (2004) also remarks on this ‘liberal democratic dilemma’. Both had in mind the exclusionary element of citizenship, but a dichotomous relation to human rights does not necessarily follow, and Habermas (2001) also looks towards the development of a ‘world citizenship’, and the cultivation at national level of an ‘obligatory cosmopolitan solidarity’ (2001: 55). In a complementary argument, Beck (2006) stresses the incorporation of human rights instruments into domestic law as an important aspect of what he calls ‘cosmopolitanization’, while Turner (1993) views universal human rights as a historical phase in the development of rights which extends beyond citizenship. It may be more accurate to say that scholars have rather used citizenship as a yardstick against which to measure the power of human rights; one example is Arendt's (1979 [1951]) treatment of ‘the right to have rights’, which considers the plight of those left stateless in the aftermath of WWI, while Soysal (1994) herself considers the limits of citizenship's exclusions in assessing the rights of trans-national migrants in contemporary Europe. While in Arendt's time there was little in the way of enforceable human rights to fall back on, today they are increasingly called upon to fill the breach where citizenship fails – either in relation to its internal guarantees, or because its external exclusions cut too deep. Quite how far human rights do fill the latter breach, and how far the state retains control in the granting of rights to non-citizens, are issues which have ignited debate about the position of trans-national migrants. None the less, another answer to the question ‘what do rights do’, as Soysal points out, is to call states to account. For Soysal, the ‘normative and cognitive authority accorded to human rights . . . exposes the limits and “incompleteness” of citizenship projects’ (2012: 17), but we are reminded by Lockwood that the ideal actualization of citizenship will always be incomplete, because it sets standards which have yet to be reached, and because our aspirations in this respect are ever expanding. I would agree with Soysal that this aspirational dynamic owes something to the force of human rights ideals, though this does not preclude the possibility of resistance at national level – whether to the extension of universal rights to non-citizens, or of welfare rights to ‘undeserving’ citizens. None the less, in noting the three registers along which human rights function – aspiration, codification, and implementation – Soysal points to the possibilities for expansion that this might open up in the practice of citizenship (see also Woodhouse 1998; Morris 2006). Indeed, human rights now become the yardstick against which citizenship is assessed, and in this connection Somers (2008) has written of the failure of Federal relief systems in the USA to address the plight of those left homeless and destitute in the wake of hurricane Katrina. Faced with such radical exclusion Somers reaches for the language of human rights, rather than that of citizenship. This creative and expansive possibility has been commonly remarked upon by scholars of human rights, so for example, Douzinas (2000) actively embraces the uncertain element of human rights, arguing that this indeterminacy has a positive aspect, granting to human rights the ability to re-imagine social boundaries. Hence, for him, human rights have the potential to create new meanings and values as novel subjects and situations lay claim to their protection, and as new rights are brought into being. We are back here to the role of normative ideals in shaping both sociological analysis and social aspiration, but I would inject a note of caution. As we have seen in the body of the article by Soysal, rights not only expand, they also contract, and social rights are perhaps the least developed area of justiciable human rights law – though this is changing (Palmer 2007). My own work has focused on the terrain of rights as a shifting terrain, and a political battlefield on which conflicting influences meet – national policy agendas, political rhetoric, judicial reasoning, and civil society mobilization; all are involved in: ‘negotiating the profile of rights which are rarely demonstrably self-evident’ (Morris 2006: 93). So my closing question is: how far are we today from a situation in which we could look to universal human rights to secure benefit rates in keeping with a dignified level of subsistence, or to rescue a generation of the young unemployed from a future of Mac-jobs and ‘training schemes’?
